intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A local developer command-line tool that scaffolds, bundles, and deploys a `deepagents` agent project — turning a project folder into a deployment artifact and either serving it locally or shipping it to the managed Deep Agents platform with the developer's own credentials. Its central promise is that the bundle faithfully and only reflects the developer's reviewed project: it must assemble only from declared sources (`agent.json`, `AGENTS.md`, `tools.json`, declared skills, subagents, and MCP servers), must never fold secrets into the seeded payload, and a deploy that would leave the agent's API open without authentication MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before proceeding. Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle must be reached over TLS, validated, and pinned to a known-good version; dependencies must ship behind a committed, pinned lockfile.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python deploy-only CLI (`deepagents-cli` 0.2.2, MIT) exposing four subcommands — `init`, `deploy`, `agents`, `mcp-servers` — against the Managed Deep Agents `/v1/deepagents/*` API; the interactive REPL was split out to the separate `deepagents-code` package at 0.1.0. `deploy/project.py` parses the project with strong primitives (symlink rejection, path-containment checks, legacy-file rejection, JSON/schema validation); `deploy/payload.py` is a pure bundler over declared sources; `deploy/api_client.py` enforces an HTTPS-only platform endpoint, uses `trust_env=False`, and ignores repo-controlled deploy state so it cannot steer authenticated requests. The bundler has no notion of frontend/anonymous-auth configuration, so the remit's unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate has no implementation; remote MCP servers are referenced by URL with no TLS-scheme check and no version/integrity pin; and `config.py` states the CLI configures no logging handlers, so no durable audit record of what was bundled or shipped exists.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is strong, real input-handling primitives around a permissive trust default: `deploy/project.py` rejects symlinks and out-of-tree paths, `api_client.py` forces an HTTPS endpoint with `trust_env=False` and refuses repo-controlled state, yet the remit's headline control — an unauthenticated-API deploy MUST warn and require operator confirmation — has no corresponding code in the bundler at all, so an anonymous-access agent ships silently. Two MCP-shaped gaps compound the theme: remote MCP server URLs are accepted and carried into the bundle with no TLS-scheme validation and no version or integrity pin (`mcp_resolver.py`, `mcp-servers add`), exactly the surfaces the remit names as MUST controls. Observability is the weakest leg: `config.py` documents that no logging handlers are configured, so the deploy path leaves no durable audit trail of what surface was shipped where.

rules.rule_text: The bundle MUST be assembled solely from the project's declared sources, and a deploy that would expose the agent's API without authentication MUST require explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.
The CLI MUST bundle only from the project's declared sources — its configuration, system prompt, skills, declared MCP servers, and declared subagents — and MUST NOT pull in undeclared content.
A deploy configured to leave the deployed API open to anyone with its URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds.
Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.
Project configuration MUST be validated before bundling, and a bundle that fails validation MUST NOT be deployed.

Credentials — deployment-platform keys, model-provider API keys, hub and tracing tokens, frontend auth secrets — MUST NOT be written into the project, committed to version control, logged, or printed.
Secret material MUST NOT be embedded into bundle artifacts that are not meant to carry it — environment files travel as environment files, never folded into the seeded-memory or skills payload.
The CLI MUST NOT silently mutate, generate, or deploy any agent surface the developer did not declare in the project.
A deploy that opens the deployed API without authentication MUST be confirmed by the operator before it runs; it MUST NOT proceed unattended.
Overwriting an existing project folder or its files during scaffolding MUST require an explicit operator opt-in.
The CLI operates under direct developer supervision as a one-shot command; it MUST NOT run as an unattended background service.
Before a deploy, the CLI MUST present the operator a clear summary of what the bundle contains and where it will be shipped — enough for the developer to recognise the surface being deployed.
A dry run MUST generate the deployment artifacts without shipping them or contacting the deployment platform's mutating endpoints.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships — confirming that the bundler carries only declared sources, that secrets never land in the seeded payload, and that the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate genuinely fires.

Authorized commands: `init` (scaffold a starter project folder), `dev` (bundle the project and run it on a local development server), and `deploy` (bundle the project and ship it to the managed deployment platform).
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, pinned to compatible ranges, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

The deployment bundle's own dependency set MUST be derived only from the project's declared model provider, declared MCP usage, declared sandbox provider, and declared auth provider — never hand-edited into the bundle out of band.
Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

findings.summary: The remit's headline unauthenticated-API confirmation gate has no implementation in the deploy bundler; an open-access agent ships silently.
findings.description: The remit makes operator confirmation of an unauthenticated-API deploy its central promise, but `execute_deploy_command` inspects no auth/frontend configuration and reaches no confirmation step keyed on access exposure — it validates the project, resolves MCP servers, and pushes. Frontend/auth is now delegated to the platform (`project.py` strips `[auth]`/`[frontend]` as platform-managed), so a deploy that leaves the deployed API open proceeds with no prominent warning and no confirmation. The gate fires only for an `agent_id`-target change, never for anonymous-access exposure.
findings.policy_rule_text: A deploy configured to leave the deployed API open to anyone with its URL MUST surface a prominent warning and obtain explicit operator confirmation before it proceeds. / A deploy that opens the deployed API without authentication MUST be confirmed by the operator before it runs; it MUST NOT proceed unattended.

evidence.snippet: execute_deploy_command, lines 256-358 — validates project, resolves MCP, and pushes; the only confirmation gate is _confirm_agent_json_target (agent_id change), none keyed on unauthenticated/open API access.
legacy-migration hint declares "[auth], [frontend] → remove; managed by the platform now" — the bundler holds no frontend/anonymous-auth notion, so no exposure-aware confirmation can fire.

recommended_actions: In `execute_deploy_command`, inspect the resolved deploy/auth configuration and, when the target would expose the agent API without authentication, print a prominent warning and require an explicit `[y/N]` confirmation (honoring `--yes`) before any mutating push.
If anonymous-access state is only knowable platform-side, fetch it before the push and gate on it; do not let an open-API deploy proceed unattended.

findings.summary: Remote MCP server URLs are carried into the bundle with no TLS-scheme validation before shipping.
findings.description: The remit requires that remote MCP servers be reached over TLS and that their transport configuration be validated before the bundle ships. `resolve_referenced_servers` only normalizes URLs (lowercase, strip trailing slash) and checks that each is registered and invokable; it never asserts an `https://` scheme. `mcp-servers add` accepts any `--url` and uses `urlparse` solely to derive a default name, so a plaintext `http://` MCP server registers and deploys without objection.
findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle MUST be reached over TLS, and their transport configuration MUST be validated before the bundle is shipped.

evidence.snippet: _normalize_url lowercases and strips trailing slash only; resolve_referenced_servers (lines 51-122) validates registration/invokability but never checks for an https scheme.
_execute_mcp_server_add accepts any --url and only urlparse()s it for a default name; no scheme/TLS validation before create_mcp_server.

recommended_actions: In `_collect_referenced_urls` / `resolve_referenced_servers`, reject (or hard-warn and require confirmation on) any referenced `mcp_server_url` whose scheme is not `https`, mirroring `_normalize_endpoint`'s HTTPS enforcement.
In `_execute_mcp_server_add` and `_execute_mcp_server_update`, validate that `--url` is `https://` before registering the server.

findings.summary: Remote MCP servers carried into the bundle are referenced by bare URL with no version or integrity pin.
findings.description: The remit requires remote MCP servers be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh. Tools reference MCP servers only by `mcp_server_url` (validated as a non-empty string in `project.py`), and the root `.mcp.json` declares `http`-type servers by URL alone — no version, digest, or fingerprint travels with the reference, so the server behind a stable URL can change silently between deploys (rug-pull surface).

tags.label: Tool definitions are signed and version-pinned

findings.policy_rule_text: Remote MCP servers declared in the project MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version, so the deployed agent does not auto-install an unpinned server afresh.

evidence.snippet: _read_tools_json requires tools[idx].mcp_server_url as a non-empty string; no version/digest/integrity field is read or required.
mcpServers docs-langchain and reference-langchain declared by url + type:http only — no version pin or integrity hash on the carried remote servers.

recommended_actions: Carry a version or content digest alongside each `mcp_server_url` in `tools.json` and validate it during `resolve_referenced_servers`, refusing a deploy whose referenced server no longer matches the pinned digest.

findings.summary: A real deploy ships the bundle before showing any summary of its contents and target; the summary prints only after the push.
findings.description: The remit requires the CLI present a clear summary of what the bundle contains and where it ships, before a deploy, so the developer can recognise the surface being deployed. `--dry-run` does print the full payload without shipping, but on a real deploy `_print_deploy_result` runs only after `create_agent`/`patch_agent` has already mutated the platform; there is no pre-push, exposure-aware summary or confirmation in the default path.
findings.policy_rule_text: Before a deploy, the CLI MUST present the operator a clear summary of what the bundle contains and where it will be shipped — enough for the developer to recognise the surface being deployed.

evidence.snippet: _deploy_agent (push) is invoked at lines 338-358 before _print_deploy_result; the summary at line 494 reports name/id/revision only after the agent has already been created or patched.
the only pre-push render is gated behind `if args.dry_run` (lines 288-302), which returns without deploying; the non-dry-run path shows no pre-deploy bundle summary.

recommended_actions: Before the mutating push in `execute_deploy_command`, print a concise summary of the bundle (files, MCP servers referenced, target endpoint/agent) and require confirmation unless `--yes` is set.

findings.summary: The shipped THREAT_MODEL.md is auto-generated and explicitly stale for this package, undercutting the remit's keep-it-current expectation.
findings.description: The remit asks the project to publish a threat model and keep it current with what the package actually ships. `THREAT_MODEL.md` carries a banner that it predates the 0.1.0 split and that its TUI/MCP/sandbox/sessions sections "no longer apply to this package," yet those out-of-date components still dominate the document; it is labelled automatically generated and not authoritative. The current in-scope deploy bundler — including the absent unauthenticated-deploy gate the remit wants the threat model to confirm — is barely covered.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD keep the threat model current with what the package actually ships — confirming that the bundler carries only declared sources, that secrets never land in the seeded payload, and that the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate genuinely fires.

evidence.snippet: banner (lines 5-12) — "generated before the 0.1.0 split... sections covering the Textual app, non_interactive.py, MCP, sandbox... no longer apply to this package."
Disclaimer — "automatically generated... experimental... not an authoritative security reference"; no threat entry confirms the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate the remit names.

recommended_actions: Regenerate `THREAT_MODEL.md` against the current `deepagents_cli.deploy` surface and add an explicit threat/control entry for the unauthenticated-API deploy path so the document confirms whether the confirmation gate exists.

findings.summary: Remit and README name a `dev` local-server command that the 0.2.0 CLI no longer ships, leaving a documented-but-absent surface.
findings.description: The remit's Known Good Baseline and the package README both list `dev` (bundle and run on a local development server) as an authorized command, but the CHANGELOG records that `deepagents dev` was removed at 0.2.0 and the parsers register only `init`, `deploy`, `agents`, and `mcp-servers`. This is doc/remit drift rather than an excess capability — the surface is narrower than declared — but it should be reconciled so the authorized-command baseline matches what ships.
findings.policy_rule_text: Authorized commands: `init` (scaffold a starter project folder), `dev` (bundle the project and run it on a local development server), and `deploy` (bundle the project and ship it to the managed deployment platform).

evidence.snippet: setup_deploy_parsers registers init/deploy/agents/mcp-servers only; no `dev` subparser exists.
0.2.0 BREAKING — "deepagents dev, deepagents.toml, and the --config/--dry-run flags are removed"; README's IMPORTANT block still lists dev as a present subcommand.

recommended_actions: Update the Worker Remit Known Good Baseline and the README to drop `dev` (or restore the command), so the authorized-command list matches the shipped parsers.

positives.title: HTTPS-only platform endpoint with environment isolation
positives.description: The deploy API client enforces an `https://` endpoint (rejecting userinfo and non-HTTPS), sets `trust_env=False`, and ignores repo-controlled deploy state, so a malicious project `.env` cannot redirect or downgrade authenticated platform traffic.

positives.title: Strict project parser — symlink rejection and path containment
positives.description: `project.py` refuses symlinks anywhere in project inputs and verifies every read path resolves inside the project root, preventing the bundle from pulling in files outside the developer's declared sources.

positives.title: MCP header values redacted on display
positives.description: `mcp-servers get` redacts header values to `***` before printing the server record, so API keys configured as auth headers are not reprinted to the terminal.

positives.title: Committed, fully hashed dependency lockfile
positives.description: A 2086-line `uv.lock` pins every transitive dependency with sha256 hashes, and Dependabot tracks the `uv` and github-actions ecosystems monthly — a reviewable, integrity-checked supply chain for the CLI itself.

positives.title: Scaffold overwrite requires explicit opt-in
positives.description: `init` refuses to overwrite an existing project folder unless `--force` is passed, satisfying the remit's approval requirement for destructive scaffolding.

log_files.no_logs_note: The CLI is a one-shot local developer command that configures no logging handlers (config.py:30 documents that `logger.warning` output is invisible to users), and no log files exist on disk or are routed from source; the absence of any durable audit record is captured in the Monitor Continuously score (1) rather than a separate finding, since the design choice is intentional for a local tool.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The CLI has a genuinely tight domain and disciplined bundling — a small four-command surface, a strict project parser, and an HTTPS-pinned, environment-isolated API client — which earns solid marks for Limit Your Domain, Balance Your Knowledge Base, and Manage Your Supply Chain (a committed, hashed `uv.lock` plus Dependabot). It is dragged down by Implement Zero Trust, where the remit's central declared control (the unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate) is simply absent and remote MCP transport is neither TLS-validated nor version-pinned, and by Monitor Continuously, where the package configures no logging and keeps no audit record of deploy actions. The result is a competent bundler with strong primitives but permissive defaults on exactly the surfaces its own remit flags as load-bearing.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The CLI exposes only `init`, `deploy`, `agents`, and `mcp-servers`, and `deploy/project.py` enforces a strict canonical layout with symlink rejection, path-containment, and legacy-file rejection; the only domain slack is doc/remit drift naming a removed `dev` command.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The bundler reads only the project's declared sources via `payload.py` (a pure function over the parsed `Project`) and never pulls undeclared content into the artifact; the CLI itself consumes no external/model/MCP content at runtime, so there is no untrusted-content-into-context surface.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Strong primitives exist — HTTPS-only endpoint enforcement, `trust_env=False`, project `.env` proxy/TLS blocking, symlink/containment guards — but the remit's headline unauthenticated-deploy confirmation gate is absent, remote MCP URLs get no TLS-scheme check, and no pre-deploy bundle summary is shown before shipping.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: A committed, fully hashed `uv.lock` pins the dependency tree and Dependabot tracks the `uv` and actions ecosystems, but `pyproject.toml` app deps are floor/caret-ranged and remote MCP servers carried into the bundle have no version or integrity pin.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A real `THREAT_MODEL.md` with an Investigated-and-Dismissed section shows adversarial analysis happened, but it is auto-generated, explicitly stale for this package (it documents the removed TUI), and the test suite is functional rather than adversarial against the bundler.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: `config.py` documents that the CLI configures no logging handlers by default, so `logger.warning` output is invisible and there is no durable, structured audit record of what was bundled or where it was deployed — only transient `print()` to stdout.
